{{Short description|Application of economics in a business}}
{{Economics sidebar}}
'''Managerial economics''' is a branch of [[economics]] involving the application of economic methods in the organizational decision-making process.<ref name=":02">* {{cite book |last1=Jones |first1=Trefor |title=Business Economics and Managerial Decision Making |date=2004 |publisher=Wiley |isbn=978-0-470-02112-5 }}{{page needed|date=April 2024}}
* {{cite book |last1=Wilkinson |first1=Nick |title=Managerial Economics: A Problem-Solving Approach |date=2005 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |isbn=978-1-139-44358-6 }}
* {{cite book |last1=Moschandreas |first1=Maria |title=Business Economics |date=2000 |publisher=Business Press |isbn=978-1-86152-399-0 }}</ref> Economics is the study of the production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services. Managerial economics involves the use of economic theories and principles to make decisions regarding the allocation of scarce resources.<ref>{{Cite book |title=Principles of Managerial Economics |publisher=Saylor Foundation |year=2016}}{{full citation needed|date=April 2024}}</ref>
It guides managers in making decisions relating to the company's customers, competitors, suppliers, and internal operations.<ref name="distanceeducationju.in">Directorate of Distance Education (no date) Directorate Of Distance Education. Available at: https://distanceeducationju.in/ (Accessed: April 23, 2023).</ref>

Managers use economic frameworks in order to optimize profits, resource allocation and the overall output of the firm, whilst improving efficiency and minimizing unproductive activities.<ref name="Banton2">{{cite web |last1=Banton |first1=Caroline |title=Business Economics |url=https://www.investopedia.com/terms/b/business-economics.asp |access-date=21 April 2021 |website=Investopdia}}</ref> These frameworks assist organizations to make rational, progressive decisions, by analyzing practical problems at both micro and macroeconomic levels.<ref name="MSQ" /> Managerial decisions involve forecasting (making decisions about the future), which involve levels of risk and uncertainty. However, the assistance of managerial economic techniques aid in informing managers in these decisions.<ref name=":22">{{cite book |last1=Beg |first1=M. A. |title=Managerial Economics |date=2010 |publisher=Ane Books Pvt Ltd |isbn=978-93-80156-92-7 }}{{page needed|date=April 2024}}</ref>

Managerial economists define managerial economics in several ways:
#It is the application of economic theory and methodology in business management practice.
#Focus on business efficiency.
#Defined as "combining economic theory with business practice to facilitate management's decision-making and forward-looking planning."
#Includes the use of an economic mindset to analyze business situations.
#Described as "a fundamental discipline aimed at understanding and analyzing business decision problems".
#Is the study of the allocation of available resources by enterprises of other management units in the activities of that unit.
#Deal almost exclusively with those business situations that can be quantified and handled, or at least quantitatively approximated, in a model.
<ref name="distanceeducationju.in"/>

'''The two main purposes of managerial economics are:'''
# To [[Optimization (mathematics)|optimize]] decision making when the firm is faced with problems or obstacles, with the consideration and application of [[Macroeconomics|macro]] and [[microeconomic]] theories and principles.<ref name="Pathak">{{cite web |last1=Pathak |first1=Ritesh |title=What is Managerial Economics? Definition, Types, Nature, Principles, and Scope |url=https://www.analyticssteps.com/blogs/what-managerial-economics-definition-types-nature-principles-scope|website=Analytic Steps |access-date=21 April 2021}}</ref>
# To analyze the possible effects and implications of both short and long-term planning decisions on the revenue and profitability of the business.

The core principles that managerial economist use to achieve the above purposes are:
* monitoring operations management and performance,
* target or goal setting
* talent management and development.

In order to optimize economic decisions, the use of [[operations research]], [[mathematical programming]], strategic decision making, [[game theory]]<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Shapiro |first1=Carl |title=The Theory of Business Strategy |journal=The RAND Journal of Economics |date=1989 |volume=20 |issue=1 |pages=125–137 |doi=10.2307/2555656 |jstor=2555656 |pmid=10296625 }}</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Webster |first1=Thomas J. |title=Managerial Economics: Theory and Practice |date=2003 |publisher=Emerald Group Publishing Limited |isbn=978-0-12-740852-1 |pages=13–14 }}</ref> and other [[Computational economics|computational methods]]<ref>For a journal on the last subject, see ''Computational Economics'', including an Aims & Scope [https://www.springer.com/economics/economic+theory/journal/10614 link]</ref> are often involved. The methods listed above are typically used for making quantitate decisions by data analysis techniques.

The theory of Managerial Economics includes a focus on; [[incentive]]s, business organization, [[Cognitive bias|biases]], [[advertising]], [[Innovation management|innovation]], [[uncertainty]], [[Pricing strategies|pricing]], [[Business analytics|analytics]], and [[Competition (economics)|competition]].<ref>W. B. Allen, Managerial Economics Theory, Applications, and Cases, 7th Edition. Norton.</ref> In other words, managerial economics is a combination of economics and managerial theory. It helps the manager in decision-making and acts as a link between practice and theory.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Baumol |first1=William J. |title=What Can Economic Theory Contribute to Managerial Economics? |journal=The American Economic Review |date=1961 |volume=51 |issue=2 |pages=142–146 |jstor=1914477 }}</ref>
Furthermore, managerial economics provides the tools and techniques that allow managers to make the optimal decisions for any scenario.

Some examples of the types of problems that the tools provided by managerial economics can answer are:
* The price and quantity of a good or service that a business should produce.
* Whether to invest in training current staff or to look into the market.
* When to purchase or retire fleet equipment.
* Decisions regarding understanding the competition between two firms based on the motive of [[profit maximization]].<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Samuelson |first1=William F. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=evwMEAAAQBAJ&dq=why+do+we+study+managerial+economics&pg=PA1 |title=Managerial Economics |last2=Marks |first2=Stephen G. |last3=Zagorsky |first3=Jay L. |date=2021-01-13 |publisher=John Wiley & Sons |isbn=978-1-119-55491-2 |language=en}}</ref>
* The impacts of consumer and competitor incentives on business decisions<ref name="Gneezy 191–210">{{cite journal |last1=Gneezy |first1=Uri |last2=Meier |first2=Stephan |last3=Rey-Biel |first3=Pedro |title=When and Why Incentives (Don't) Work to Modify Behavior |journal=Journal of Economic Perspectives |date=November 2011 |volume=25 |issue=4 |pages=191–210 |doi=10.1257/jep.25.4.191 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

Managerial economics is sometimes referred to as [[business economics]] and is a branch of [[economics]] that applies [[microeconomic]] analysis to decision methods of businesses or other management units to assist managers to make a wide array of multifaceted decisions. The calculation and quantitative analysis draws heavily from techniques such as [[regression analysis]], [[correlation]] and [[calculus]].<ref>NA (2009). "managerial economics," ''Encyclopædia Britannica''.  [http://www.britannica.com/EBchecked/topic/361242/managerial-economics+%%22 Cached online entry.]{{dead link|date=September 2024|bot=medic}}</ref>

==Economic Theories relevant to Managerial Economics==

Microeconomics is the dominant focus behind managerial economics, some of the key aspects include:

* ''Supply and Demand''

[[File:Supply and Demand curve.png|thumb|Supply and Demand Relationship]]

The law of supply and demand describes the relationship between producers and consumers of a product.<ref name="Fernando">{{cite web |last1=Fernando |first1=Jason |title=Law of Supply and Demand |url=https://www.investopedia.com/terms/l/law-of-supply-demand.asp |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=Investopedia}}</ref> The law suggests that price set by the producer and quantity demanded by a consumer are inversely proportional, meaning an increase in the price set is met by a reduction in demand by the consumer.<ref name="Fernando" />
The law further describes that sellers will produce a larger quantity of the good if it sells at a higher price.<ref name="Fernando" />

Excess demand exists when the quantity of a good demanded is greater than the quantity supplied. Where there is excess demand, sellers can benefit by increasing the price. The inverse applies to excess supply.

* ''[[Production theory]]''

Production theory describes the quantity of a good a business chooses to produce.<ref name="Theory of Production">{{cite web |last1=Dorfman |first1=Robert |title=Theory of Production |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/theory-of-production |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=Britannica}}</ref> This decision is informed by a variety of factors, including raw material inputs, labor, and capital costs like machinery.<ref name="Theory of Production" /> The production theory states that a business will strive to employ the cheapest combination of inputs to produce the quantity demanded.
The production function can be described in its simplest form by the function <math>Q = F[L,K]</math> where {{mvar|Q}} denotes the firm's production, {{mvar|L}} is the variable inputs and {{mvar|K}} is the fixed inputs.

* ''[[Opportunity cost]]''

The opportunity cost of a choice is the foregone benefit of the second best choice.<ref>{{cite book |doi=10.1007/978-1-349-21315-3_69 |chapter=Opportunity Cost |title=The World of Economics |date=1991 |last1=Buchanan |first1=James M. |pages=520–525 |isbn=978-0-333-55177-6 }}</ref> Determining the opportunity cost requires detailing the costs and benefits of each action the business is considering to pursue, and the cost of choosing one activity over another.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Henderson |first1=David |title=Opportunity Cost |url=https://www.econlib.org/library/Enc/OpportunityCost.html |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=The Library of Economics and Liberty}}</ref> The decision-maker is then in the position to choose the action with the highest payoff.

* ''Theory of Exchange or Price Theory''

The principle uses the conjecture of supply and demand to set an accurate price for a good.<ref name="Theory of Price">{{cite web |last1=Banton |first1=Caroline |title=Theory of Price |url=https://www.investopedia.com/terms/t/theory-of-price.asp#:~:text=The%20theory%20of%20price%20is,between%20its%20supply%20and%20demand. |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=Investopedia}}</ref> The aim of price theory is to allocate a price for a good such that the supply of a good is met with equal demand for the product.<ref name="Theory of Price" /> If a manager sets the price of a good too high, the consumer may think it is not worth the cost and decide not to purchase the good, hence creating excess supply. The opposite occurs when the price is set too low, causing demand for a good to be greater than supply.<ref name="Theory of Price" />

* ''Theory of Capital and Investment Decisions''

Capital investment decisions are a critical factor in an enterprise. They involve determining the rational allocation of funds that will enable an organization to invest in profitable projects or enterprises to improve the efficiency of organizations.<ref name="Capital Investment Decisions">{{cite web |last1=Bragg |first1=Steven |title=Capital Investment Decisions |url=https://www.accountingtools.com/articles/capital-investment-decisions.html#:~:text=Capital%20investment%20decisions%20involve%20the,spent%20to%20procure%20capital%20assets.&text=Whether%20the%20cash%20flows%20from,a%20positive%20return%20on%20investment. |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=Accounting Tools}}</ref>
The rational allocation of funds may include acquiring business, investing in equipment, or determining whether an investment will improve the business at all.<ref name="Capital Investment Decisions" />

* ''[[Price Elasticity of Demand|Elasticity of demand]]''

The elasticity of demand is a prominent concept in managerial economics. Established by Alfred Marshall, elasticity of demand describes how sensitive a change in the quantity demanded is to a unit change in price. In his own words, Marshall describes the concept as: "The elasticity of demand in a market is great or small according to whether the amount demanded increases much or little for a given fall in price, and diminishes much or little for a given rise in price."<ref>{{Cite web |date=2016-05-23 |title=Top 14 Contributions of Alfred Marshall to Economics |url=https://www.economicsdiscussion.net/economics-2/alfred-marshall/top-14-contributions-of-alfred-marshall-to-economics/21044 |access-date=2022-03-22 |website=Economics Discussion |language=en-US}}</ref>

Microeconomic principles are useful in informing managers' decision-making. Managerial economics draws upon all of these analytical tools to make informed business decisions.

==Analytical Methods used in Managerial Economics==

* ''[[Price Elasticity of Demand]] Analysis''

The price elasticity of demand is a highly useful tool in managerial economics as it provides managers with the predicted change in demand associated with an increase in the price charged for its goods and services.<ref name="investopedia.com">{{cite web |last1=Hall |first1=Mary |title=Elasticity vs. Inelasticity of Demand: What's the Difference? |url=https://www.investopedia.com/ask/answers/012915/what-difference-between-inelasticity-and-elasticity-demand.asp |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=Investopedia}}</ref> The price elasticity principle also outlines the changes in demand for goods with changes in the income of a populous.<ref name="investopedia.com" />

<math>Elasticity(p) = E_{\langle p \rangle} = \frac{\Delta Q/Q}{\Delta P/P}</math>

Where <math>\Delta Q</math> is the change in quantity demand for the respective change in price <math>\Delta P</math>, with {{mvar|Q}} and {{mvar|P}} representing the quantity and price of the good before a change was made.<ref name="dummies.com">{{cite web |last1=Graham |first1=Robert |title=How to Calculate Price Elasticity of Demand with Calculus |url=https://www.dummies.com/education/economics/how-to-calculate-price-elasticity-of-demand-with-calculus/ |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=Dummies}}</ref>
The price elasticity is important for managerial economics as it aids in the optimization of marginal revenue of firms.<ref name="dummies.com" />

* ''[[Marginal analysis]]''

In economics, marginal refers to the change in revenue and cost by producing one extra unit of output. Both the marginal cost and marginal revenue are extremely important in economics as a firm's profit is maximized when the marginal cost is equal to the marginal revenue.<ref>{{cite web |title=Marginal Revenue and Marginal Cost |url=https://www.core-econ.org/the-economy/book/text/leibniz-07-06-01.html |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=The Economy}}</ref> Managers can make business decisions on the output level based on this analysis in order to maximize the profit of the firm.

Marginal Analysis is considered one of the chief tools in managerial economics, as it involves a comparison between marginal benefits and  marginal costs to arrive at optimal variable decisions. Managerial economics uses explanatory variables such as output, price, product quality, advertising, and research and development to maximise net benefits.

* ''[[Mathematical model]] analysis''

The use of econometric analysis has grown with the  development of economics and management, as has the use of differential calculus to determine profit maximisation.<ref>{{cite web |title=Optimisation Techniques |url=https://www.shsu.edu/~eco_dgf/web_chapter_a.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20040702180850/https://www.shsu.edu/~eco_dgf/web_chapter_a.pdf |archive-date=2004-07-02 |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=Web Chapter}}</ref>

By taking the derivative of a function, the maximum and minimum values of the function are easily determined by setting the derivative equal to zero. This can be applied to a production function to find the quantity of production that maximizes the profit of the firm.<ref>{{cite web |title=Optimisation Techniques |url=https://www.shsu.edu/~eco_dgf/web_chapter_a.pdf |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=Web Chapter}}</ref> This concept is important for managers to understand in order to minimize costs or maximize profits.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Dutta |first1=Nikita |date=15 April 2015 |title=Functions of Optimization (6 Functions With Diagram) |url=https://www.economicsdiscussion.net/optimization/functions-of-optimization-6-functions-with-diagram/6146 |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=Economics Discussion}}</ref>

The main applications of mathematical models are:

* ''Demand forecasting''. Before determining the scale of production of a certain product, enterprises need to forecast the development potential of the market. Relevant mathematical models can be created to represent the quantitative changes in the various factors affecting the development of the market, and then analyse the magnitude of the impact of these changes on demand.
* ''Production analysis''. The input of production factors, the choice of the form of production organisation and the determination of the product structure can all be analysed and decided by creating mathematical models.
* ''Cost decision''. Cost is a factor that directly affects profit, and is one of the most important concerns for enterprise development. An enterprise's cost level can be determined by applying mathematical models. When an enterprise changes the direction of production and operation, or expands its scale these methods can help determine the optimal level under the goal of maximising profit. 
* ''[[Market analysis]]''. The market is a fundamental concept in economics and in practice manifests itself in many different forms. Mathematical models can be created to analyse the size, price and competitive strategies that a company may choose under various market conditions.
* ''Risk analysis''. Risk analysis is the prediction of future states. Mathematical models can be created to represent the magnitude of the various factors involved in an investment and the impact that changes in magnitude may have on the benefits.

==Decision Making in Managerial Economics==

As "the application of economic theory and methods to business decision-making",<ref name=":7" /> managerial economics is fundamentally about making decisions. The discipline is partially prescriptive in nature because it suggests a course of action to a managerial problem.<ref name="Banton2"/> Managerial economics aims to provide the tools and techniques to make informed decisions to maximize the profits and minimize the losses of a firm.<ref name="Banton2"/> Managerial economics has use in many different business applications, although the most common focus areas are related to the risk, pricing, production and capital decisions a manager makes.<ref name="relivingmbadays">{{cite web |title=Concept of Managerial Economics |url=https://relivingmbadays.wordpress.com/2012/11/06/concept-of-managerial-economics/ |website=relivingmbadays |date=6 November 2012 |access-date=22 April 2021}}</ref> Managers study managerial economics because it gives them the insight to control the operations of their organizations. Organizations will function well if managers rationally apply the principles that apply to economic behavior.<ref name="distanceeducationju.in"/>
[[File:Business Decision Making Framework.png|thumb|]]

'''Managerial economics as a science'''

# ''Define the Problem''<br />The first step in making a business decision is to understand the problem in its entirety. Without correct analysis of the problem, any solution developed will be inadequate.<ref name="Lucidchart">{{cite web |title=7 Steps of the Decision-Making Process |url=https://www.lucidchart.com/blog/decision-making-process-steps |website=Lucidchart |date=7 December 2017 |access-date=22 April 2021}}</ref><br />Incorrect problem identification can sometimes cause the problem that is trying to be solved.<ref name="theintactone.com">{{cite web |title=Role of Managerial Economics in Decision Making |url=https://theintactone.com/2019/10/13/me-u1-topic-3-role-of-managerial-economics-in-decision-making/ |website=THEINTACTONE |date=13 October 2019 |access-date=25 April 2021}}</ref>
# ''Determine the Objective''<br />The second step is evaluating the objective of the decision, or what the decision is trying to achieve.<ref name="theintactone.com"/> <br />This step is determining a possible solution to the problem defined in step 1. Multiple possible solutions to the problem previously identified may be established. 
# ''Discover the Alternatives''<br />After in depth analysis into what is required to solve the problem faced by a business, options for potential solutions can be collated.<ref name="Lucidchart"/><br />In most cases, more than one possible solution to the problem exists. For example, a business striving to gain more attraction on social media could improve the quality of their content, collaborate with other creators or a combination of the two.<ref name="Lucidchart"/>
# ''Forecast the Consequences''<br />This step involves assessing the consequences of the problem's solutions detailed in step 3. Possible consequences of a business decisions could include; productivity, health, environmental impacts and risk.<ref name="Davis">{{cite web |last1=Davis |first1=Marc |title=Identifying and Managing Business Risks |url=https://www.investopedia.com/articles/financial-theory/09/risk-management-business.asp |website=Investopedia |access-date=22 April 2021}}</ref><br />Here, managerial economics is used to determine the risks and potential financial consequences of an action.
# ''Make a Decision''<br />After the consequences and potential solutions to the problem at hand have been analyzed, a decision can be made. At this point, the potential decisions should be measurable values which have been quantified by managerial economics to maximise profits and minimise risk and adverse outcomes of the firm.<ref name="theintactone.com"/> This step includes a sensitivity analysis of the solution. A sensitivity analysis of the selected solution details how the output of the solution changes with changes to the inputs.<ref name="Maverick">{{cite web |last1=Maverick |first1=J.B. |title=How Is Sensitivity Analysis Used? |url=https://www.investopedia.com/ask/answers/052115/what-are-some-examples-ways-sensitivity-analysis-can-be-used.asp |website=Investopedia |access-date=22 April 2021}}</ref> The sensitivity analysis allows the strengths and weaknesses of the designed solution to be analyzed.<ref name="theintactone.com"/>

== Pricing ==
It is important to understand what pricing decisions should be made regarding the products and services of the firm. Efficient pricing is required to maintain desired levels of revenue and profit, whilst also maintaining customer satisfaction.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Reekie |first1=W. Duncan |title=Pricing: Making profitable decisions, Kent B. Monroe, pricing: Making profitable decisions, McGraw-Hill, New York, 1979. pp. xv+286. £8.50 |journal=Managerial and Decision Economics |date=March 1980 |volume=1 |issue=1 |pages=46–47 |doi=10.1002/mde.4090010110 }}</ref> Setting a price too low reduces profitability, negatively affects the perceived quality of the product, and sets an expectation of price for the consumer. Setting a price too high may negatively affect the image of an organisation from the perspective of the consumer.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Fournier |first=Susan |date=March 1998 |title=Consumers and Their Brands: Developing Relationship Theory in Consumer Research |journal=Journal of Consumer Research |volume=24 |issue=4 |pages=343–353 |doi=10.1086/209515 }}</ref>

Managers may price using intuitive or technocratic decision-making styles. A technocratic approach relies on quantitative analysis and optimisation, and typically involves a compensatory method of evaluation.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Rusetski |first=Alexander |date=August 2014 |title=Pricing by intuition: Managerial choices with limited information |journal=Journal of Business Research |volume=67 |issue=8 |pages=1733–1743 |doi=10.1016/j.jbusres.2014.02.020 }}</ref> Compensatory evaluation allows one attribute to compensate for another attribute. For example, a manager may price a product at a lower price to compensate for its lower quality.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Katsikopoulos |first=Konstantinos V. |date=March 2011 |title=Psychological Heuristics for Making Inferences: Definition, Performance, and the Emerging Theory and Practice |journal=Decision Analysis |volume=8 |issue=1 |pages=10–29 |doi=10.1287/deca.1100.0191 }}</ref> Intuitive decision-making relies on consumer [[heuristics]], defined as cognitive processes of fast decision-making, which occur by limiting the amount of information analysed.<ref>{{cite book |doi=10.1093/acprof:oso/9780199744282.003.0001 |hdl=11858/00-001M-0000-0024-F678-0 |hdl-access=free |chapter=Homo heuristicus: Why Biased Minds Make Better Inferences |title=Heuristics |date=2011 |last1=Gigerenzer |first1=Gerd |last2=Brighton |first2=Henry |pages=2–26 |isbn=978-0-19-974428-2 }}</ref>

Economic concepts such as [[competitive advantage]], [[market segmentation]], and [[price discrimination]] are relevant to pricing strategy.<ref name=":7"/> In order to set a price that drives sales and firm performance, managers must understand the economic environment in which they are operating.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Nagle |first1=Thomas |title=Economic Foundations for Pricing |journal=The Journal of Business |date=1984 |volume=57 |issue=1 |pages=S3–S26 |doi=10.1086/296232 |jstor=2352919 }}</ref>

=== Price Discrimination ===

[[Price discrimination]] involves selling the same or similar good at different prices to different consumer segments.<ref>{{Cite book |last1=Robinson |first1=Joan |title=The economics of imperfect competition |date=2002 |publisher=Palgrave |isbn=0-333-98676-8 |oclc=50433585}}</ref> Consumer segments are separated by a significant variation in the amount they are willing to pay. In order for price discrimination to occur, firms must be able to separate customer segments according to differing price elasticities, have some market power and prevent customers from re-selling the product.<ref name="Varian 597–654">{{cite book |doi=10.1016/s1573-448x(89)01013-7 |chapter=Price discrimination |title=Handbook of Industrial Organization Volume 1 |date=1989 |last1=Varian |first1=Hal R. |volume=1 |pages=597–654 |isbn=978-0-444-70434-4 }}</ref>

There are three classic types of price discrimination.

* ''First-degree price discrimination'' or perfect price discrimination occurs when firm's can accurately determine what each buyer is willing to pay. However, in practice this strategy is difficult to achieve as it requires full knowledge of the demand curve.<ref name=":7">{{cite book |doi=10.1017/CBO9780511810534.016 |chapter=Pricing strategy |title=Managerial Economics |date=2005 |pages=382–429 |isbn=978-0-521-81993-0 |first1=Nick |last1=Wilkinson }}</ref>
* ''Second-degree price discrimination'' occurs when firms can price products or services differently according to the number of units bought. Examples include quantity discounting, bulk pricing and two-for-one offers.<ref name="Varian 597–654"/>
* ''Third-degree price discrimination'' prices products or services differently based on the unique demographic of different groups. Examples of third-degree price discrimination are student or senior discounts, or discounted travel tickets for last-minute buys.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |title=3 Degrees of Price Discrimination |url=https://www.investopedia.com/ask/answers/042415/what-are-different-types-price-discrimination-and-how-are-they-used.asp |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=Investopedia |language=en}}</ref>
Additional forms of price discrimination include bundling, intrapersonal price discrimination and purchase-history price discrimination.<ref>{{cite book |doi=10.1016/S1573-448X(06)03034-2 |chapter=Price Discrimination and Competition |title=Handbook of Industrial Organization Volume 3 |date=2007 |last1=Stole |first1=Lars A. |volume=3 |pages=2221–2299 |isbn=978-0-444-82435-6 }}</ref> A firm's ability to price discriminate effectively can improve their profitability and/or increase their customer base, but only if the conditions required for price discrimination are met.

=== Psychology of Pricing ===
The Psychology of [[Pricing]] is used to understand how pricing affects consumers perception of goods and their willingness to consume. The way a good is priced has implications for the perceived value of that good. Firms can capitalise on consumers willingness to pay by influencing their price perception, reducing the pain of paying and exploiting switching costs.

Consumer's price perception can be altered by [[Psychological pricing|priming a smaller number]] (e.g. pricing a good as $4.99 instead of $5), anchoring to a high reference price or separating costs into individual components (e.g. the price of the good and shipping cost).

Reducing the pain of paying involves strategies to minimise the psychological "pain" individuals feel when spending, due to human's [[Loss aversion|loss averse]] nature.<ref>{{Cite web |last=BehavioralEconomics.com |title=Pain of paying |url=https://www.behavioraleconomics.com/resources/mini-encyclopedia-of-be/pain-of-paying/ |access-date=2023-04-20 |website=BehavioralEconomics.com {{!}} The BE Hub |language=en-US}}</ref> These include timing strategies, like block payments or charging before consumption, and salience strategies like digital or artificial (e.g. tokens) payments.

Finally, by exploiting [[Switching barriers|switching costs]], firms can increase producer surplus and/or keep a larger market share. Switching costs "result from a consumers desire for compatibility between a current purchase and previous investment".<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Klemperer |first=Paul |date=1995 |title=Competition when Consumers have Switching Costs: An Overview with Applications to Industrial Organization, Macroeconomics, and International Trade |journal=The Review of Economic Studies |volume=62 |issue=4 |pages=515–539 |doi=10.2307/2298075 |jstor=2298075 }}</ref> However, often consumers fail to switch to the optimal choice because of [[loss aversion]], information deficiencies, [[procrastination]], [[Status quo bias|status-quo bias]] or [[endowment effect]]s.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Karle |first1=Heiko |last2=Schumacher |first2=Heiner |last3=Vølund |first3=Rune |title=Consumer loss aversion and scale-dependent psychological switching costs |journal=Games and Economic Behavior |date=March 2023 |volume=138 |pages=214–237 |doi=10.1016/j.geb.2023.01.001 |doi-access=free }}</ref> This allows firms to exploit this behaviour through strategies such as honeymoon pricing (or introductory rates) and add-on pricing, which involves a cheaper initial purchase but more expensive replacement (like printers and cartridges).

The psychology of pricing provides an explanation for why consumption patterns don't always cohere with the neoclassical understanding, which assumes price and consumption have an inverse relationship.<ref name=":5">{{cite book |last1=Oh |first1=Haemoon |last2=Pizam |first2=Abraham |title=Handbook of Hospitality Marketing Management |date=2008 |publisher=Elsevier |isbn=978-0-08-045080-3 }}{{page needed|date=April 2024}}</ref>  The Snob Effect, Bandwagon Effect and Veblen Effect are three counter-examples to this assumption.

* ''The [[Snob effect|Snob Effect]]'' occurs when consumers reduce their consumption of a good when others begin consuming it.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last1=Uzgoren |first1=Ergin |last2=Guney |first2=Taner |date=2012 |title=The Snob Effect in the Consumption of Luxury Goods |journal=Procedia - Social and Behavioral Sciences |volume=62 |pages=628–637 |doi=10.1016/j.sbspro.2012.09.105 |doi-access=free }}</ref> Have attached unique perceived value to the good, this value erodes as demand increases.
* The [[Bandwagon effect|Bandwagon Effect]] is the inverse. Consumers want to consume what everyone else is and therefore value goods by perceived social value rather than price.<ref name=":5" />
* Under ''the [[Veblen good|Veblen Effect]]'', consumers accept price as an indication of value, and therefore consume more as the price increases.<ref name=":6" />

== Consumer decision making (Theories and Biases) ==

In order to successfully make organisational decisions, management must have an understanding of [[consumer behaviour]] and decision-making. Consumer behaviour relates to buying, using and selling goods, services, time and ideas by decision-making units.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Jacoby |first1=Jacob |title=Consumer psychology as a social psychological sphere of action. |journal=American Psychologist |date=October 1975 |volume=30 |issue=10 |pages=977–987 |doi=10.1037/0003-066x.30.10.977 }}</ref>

=== Rational Choice Theory ===

[[Rational choice theory|Rational Choice Theory]] is a decision-making theory, also known as the law-and-economics theory, which applies the assumption that people will try and maximise their outcomes, have well-defined preferences and are consistently rational decision-makers.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bekir |first1=Insaf |last2=Doss |first2=Faten |title=Status quo bias and attitude towards risk: An experimental investigation |journal=Managerial and Decision Economics |date=July 2020 |volume=41 |issue=5 |pages=827–838 |doi=10.1002/mde.3140 }}</ref> This theory develops on the Economic Man Theory, which assumed that people respond to stimuli (external factors) to generate a response (outcome). Rational Choice Theory builds on this theory by understanding that the consumer is an information processing decision-maker, however, it fails to incorporate psychological literature and empirical findings on the psychology of human-behaviour.<ref name=":52">{{cite journal |last1=Jacoby |first1=Jacob |title=It It Rational to Assume Consumer Rationality? Some Consumer Psychological Perspectives on Rational Choice Theory |journal=Roger Williams University Law Review |date=5 January 2000 |volume=6 |issue=1 |ssrn=239538 |url=https://docs.rwu.edu/rwu_LR/vol6/iss1/5/ }}</ref>

Rational Choice Theory makes the following assumptions:

# Objective criteria exist to enable a consumer to determine rational choices from irrational choices.
# Organisations and Consumers have negligible behavioural differences.
# Consumers make decisions based on conscious consideration of factors.
# Consumers make decisions using rational considerations.
# Consumers decide from a stable set of preferences.
# Consumers aim to maximise their circumstances.
# In maximising their circumstances, consumers perform a risk assessment.
# Satisfaction is easily assessable.

These assumptions do not account for circumstances of human error where consumers misinterpret information, or only consider portions of relevant information. The assumption of rational choice theory that when provided with all the required information, consumers will make a rational decision is limited.<ref name=":52" /> Instead, understanding [[bounded rationality]], a concept explored in [[behavioural economics]], can assist firms and managers in decision making.

=== State-Dependent Preferences ===

Consumer preferences depend on the state the consumer is in when making the decision. For example, food tastes better when you are hungry or attending a concert is more enjoyable if you are not injured.<ref name="Haggag 2136–2183">{{cite journal |last1=Haggag |first1=Kareem |last2=Pope |first2=Devin G |last3=Bryant-Lees |first3=Kinsey B |last4=Bos |first4=Maarten W |title=Attribution Bias in Consumer Choice |journal=The Review of Economic Studies |date=October 2019 |volume=86 |issue=5 |pages=2136–2183 |doi=10.1093/restud/rdy054 }}{{Retracted|doi=10.1093/restud/rdy057|intentional=yes}}</ref> Most models of state-dependant preferences assume people are aware of the effect their current state has on preference formation in that moment. However, empirical studies suggest this is not always true.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Loewenstein |first1=G. |last2=O'Donoghue |first2=T. |last3=Rabin |first3=M. |title=Projection Bias in Predicting Future Utility |journal=The Quarterly Journal of Economics |date=November 2003 |volume=118 |issue=4 |pages=1209–1248 |doi=10.1162/003355303322552784 |url=https://escholarship.org/uc/item/5qh6142m }}</ref> Several biases help explain this incongruence.

* ''Projection bias'' occurs when consumers predict that their future tastes will represent their current tastes.
* ''[[Attribution bias]]'' occurs when consumers consider past experiences in deciding whether to repeat a previously performed consumption activity. This bias can lead to systematic errors in economic decisions.<ref name="Haggag 2136–2183" />
* ''Status quo bias'' occurs when consumers would rather follow previous procedures, or buy previously used products, without evidence that this choice is better than alternatives.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Thaler |first1=Richard H. |last2=Benartzi |first2=Shlomo |title=Save More Tomorrow™: Using Behavioral Economics to Increase Employee Saving |journal=Journal of Political Economy |date=February 2004 |volume=112 |issue=S1 |pages=S164–S187 |doi=10.1086/380085 }}</ref> This bias opposes the basic law of human nature, that the most adaptable species is the one to survive, and is in opposition with positive views on change expressed in business literature.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ali Al Qudah |first1=Mohammad |title=The Impact of Entrepreneurship Initiatives in Enhancing Creativity and Innovation |journal=International Journal of Business and Management |date=17 June 2018 |volume=13 |issue=7 |pages=157 |doi=10.5539/ijbm.v13n7p157 |s2cid=242086961 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

=== Cognitive Biases ===
Beyond biases that influence state-dependent preferences, there is a whole host of cognitive biases that can shape consumer preferences and influence decision making.<ref>{{Cite web |title=List of Cognitive Biases and Heuristics |url=https://thedecisionlab.com/biases |access-date=2023-04-06 |website=The Decision Lab |language=en-CA}}</ref> See [[List of cognitive biases|cognitive biases]] for a full list. These biases can have real implications for the effectiveness of firms, public policies and choices generally.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Orphanides |first=Athanasios |date=2015 |title=Fear of Liftoff: Uncertainty, Rules, and Discretion in Monetary Policy Normalization |journal=Review |volume=97 |issue=3 |doi=10.20955/r.97.173-96 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

== Incentives ==

Monetary and non-monetary incentives are used by managers to motivate employees to achieve results aligned with firms' objectives.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ballentine |first1=Andrew |last2=McKenzie |first2=Nora |last3=Wysocki |first3=Allen |last4=Kepner |first4=Karl |date=1969-12-31 |title=The Role of Monetary and Non-Monetary Incentives in the Workplace as Influenced by Career Stage |journal=EDIS |volume=2003 |issue=8 |doi=10.32473/edis-hr016-2003 |issn=2576-0009|doi-access=free }}</ref> The outcome of incentives depends on the design and the implementation process of the incentives, their interaction with intrinsic and social motivations, and the behavioural effects of their removal.<ref name="Gneezy 191–210"/>

In a field experiment analyzing the effects of performance-based monetary incentives, it was shown that productivity improved in line with employees' ability, however, there was an increase in neglect of non-incentivised tasks.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kishore |first1=Sunil |last2=Rao |first2=Raghunath Singh |last3=Narasimhan |first3=Om |last4=John |first4=George |title=Bonuses versus Commissions: A Field Study |journal=Journal of Marketing Research |date=June 2013 |volume=50 |issue=3 |pages=317–333 |doi=10.1509/jmr.11.0485 |s2cid=167436619 }}</ref>

Monetary incentives generally have two kinds of effects, known as the standard direct price effect, and the indirect psychological effect. The standard direct price effect makes incentivised behavior more attractive; and the indirect psychological effect makes incentivised behavior less appealing by relaying important information from principals (manager) to agents (worker) surrounding quality expectations, which can provoke unexpected behavioural outcomes.<ref name="Gneezy 191–210" />

Agents receive information from both the size and existence of incentives. For example, offering members of the community high monetary compensation to be in the presence of a nuclear waste site, indicates that there are high risks involved with the plant, making community members less willing to accept the plant even in the presence of monetary incentives.<ref>{{cite book |doi=10.15358/9783800650620-66 |chapter=8. Lokal unerwünschte Projekte (Mit FELIX OBERHOLZER-GEE) |title=Markt und Motivation |date=1997 |last1=Frey |first1=Bruno S. |pages=66–74 |isbn=978-3-8006-5062-0 }}</ref> Contrarily, in a famous experiment, a childcare centre introduced a fee of $3 for parents who picked their children up late. The information conveyed to the parents from this incentive was that the small fine indicated being late is not too bad, and in the short run the number of late pick-ups increased. This information persisted when the fee was removed, and parents who had experienced the fine were more likely to pick their child up late than those who had not received the information given by the incentive.<ref>{{cite book |first1=Uri |last1=Gneezy |first2=Aldo |last2=Rustichini |chapter=Incentives, Punishment, and Behavior |pages=572–589 |doi=10.2307/j.ctvcm4j8j.26 |jstor=j.ctvcm4j8j.26 |title=Advances in Behavioral Economics |date=2004 |publisher=Princeton University Press |isbn=978-0-691-11681-5 }}</ref>

As a general rule however, when incentives are high enough, the standard direct price effect tends to take precedence over the indirect psychological effect, unless incentives are so high that agents form a negative inference of the circumstances.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ariely |first1=Dan |last2=Gneezy |first2=Uri |last3=Loewenstein |first3=George |last4=Mazar |first4=Nina |title=Large Stakes and Big Mistakes |journal=Review of Economic Studies |date=April 2009 |volume=76 |issue=2 |pages=451–469 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-937X.2009.00534.x |hdl=10419/55603 |url=http://www.bostonfed.org/economic/wp/wp2005/wp0511.pdf |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

=== Pay disparity ===

Where workers are paid at a substantially lower rate to their peers, outputs and attendance can fall out of alignment with organisational objectives. Pay disparity can cause harm to an organisations social culture, cohesion and cooperation, and alter the workplace dynamic significantly. In developing countries where social interactions are heavily relied upon for economic activity, these effects are particularly undesirable.<ref name="Breza">{{cite report |doi=10.3386/w22491 |title=The Morale Effects of Pay Inequality |date=2016 |last1=Breza |first1=Emily |last2=Kaur |first2=Supreet |last3=Shamdasani |first3=Yogita |doi-access=free }}</ref> Evidence suggests these consequences can be avoided by clearly justifying pay inequality to workers. Potentially due to self-serving bias, workers are often unwilling to believe they perform at a lower standard than their peers unless shown undeniable evidence.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Fang |first1=Hanming |last2=Moscarini |first2=Giuseppe |title=Morale hazard |journal=Journal of Monetary Economics |date=May 2005 |volume=52 |issue=4 |pages=749–777 |doi=10.1016/j.jmoneco.2005.02.001 |s2cid=17801049 }}</ref> Particularly in settings where employees do not trust their managers, workers may be inclined to suspect favouritism until they are given evidence and justifications.<ref name="Breza" />

[[Tournament theory]] is used to describe why different pay levels exist between different roles in the business hierarchy. The idea of tournament theory is that agents who put in effort to achieve promotions are rewarded with a higher, non-incremental, pay rate. The reward of a higher pay rate incentivizes behaviour that leads to promotions. This behaviour is often lucrative and therefore ideal for the business.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Lazear |first1=Edward P. |last2=Shaw |first2=Kathryn L. |title=Personnel Economics: The Economist's View of Human Resources |journal=The Journal of Economic Perspectives |date=2007 |volume=21 |issue=4 |pages=91–114 |doi=10.1257/jep.21.4.91  |doi-access=free|jstor=30033753 |url=http://papers.nber.org/papers/w13653.pdf }}</ref> Tournaments can be very powerful at incentivizing performance. Empirical research in economics and managements have shown that tournament-like incentive structure increases the individual performance of workers and managers in the workplace.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Sheremeta |first=Roman M. |date=2016-10-01 |title=The pros and cons of workplace tournaments |url=https://wol.iza.org/articles/pros-and-cons-of-workplace-tournaments/long |journal=IZA World of Labor |language=en-US |doi=10.15185/izawol.302 |doi-access=free|hdl=10419/148527 |hdl-access=free }}</ref>

However, research has also shown that tournaments consistently disadvantage certain groups, such as women.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Niederle |first1=M. |last2=Vesterlund |first2=L. |title=Do Women Shy Away From Competition? Do Men Compete Too Much? |journal=The Quarterly Journal of Economics |date=August 2007 |volume=122 |issue=3 |pages=1067–1101 |doi=10.1162/qjec.122.3.1067 |url=http://papers.nber.org/papers/w11474.pdf }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gneezy |first1=U. |last2=Niederle |first2=M. |last3=Rustichini |first3=A. |title=Performance in Competitive Environments: Gender Differences |journal=The Quarterly Journal of Economics |date=August 2003 |volume=118 |issue=3 |pages=1049–1074 |doi=10.1162/00335530360698496 }}</ref> The prevalence of tournament structured competitions for career progression provides an explanation for why women are often underrepresented in high positions. Other studies have investigated gender discrepancies in [[risk aversion]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Borghans |first1=Lex |last2=Golsteyn |first2=Bart H. H. |last3=Heckman |first3=James J. |last4=Meijers |first4=Huub |title=Gender Differences in Risk Aversion and Ambiguity Aversion |journal=Journal of the European Economic Association |date=April 2009 |volume=7 |issue=2–3 |pages=649–658 |doi=10.1162/JEEA.2009.7.2-3.649 |url=http://www.nber.org/papers/w14713.pdf }}</ref> feedback aversion,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Charness |first1=Gary |last2=Gneezy |first2=Uri |title=Strong Evidence for Gender Differences in Risk Taking |journal=Journal of Economic Behavior & Organization |date=June 2012 |volume=83 |issue=1 |pages=50–58 |doi=10.1016/j.jebo.2011.06.007 }}</ref> [[Overconfidence effect|overconfidence]],<ref>{{cite journal |last1=van Veldhuizen |first1=Roel |title=Gender Differences in Tournament Choices: Risk Preferences, Overconfidence, or Competitiveness? |journal=Journal of the European Economic Association |date=11 August 2022 |volume=20 |issue=4 |pages=1595–1618 |doi=10.1093/jeea/jvac031 |hdl=10419/261094 |hdl-access=free }}</ref> self-perception of ability,<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Heatherington |first1=Laurie |last2=Burns |first2=Andrea B. |last3=Gustafson |first3=Timothy B. |title=When Another Stumbles: Gender and Self-Presentation to Vulnerable Others |journal=Sex Roles |date=1998 |volume=38 |issue=11/12 |pages=889–913 |doi=10.1023/A:1018866307680 |s2cid=92980904 }}</ref> [[negotiation]] skills<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bowles |first1=Hannah Riley |last2=Babcock |first2=Linda |last3=Lai |first3=Lei |title=Social incentives for gender differences in the propensity to initiate negotiations: Sometimes it does hurt to ask |journal=Organizational Behavior and Human Decision Processes |date=May 2007 |volume=103 |issue=1 |pages=84–103 |doi=10.1016/j.obhdp.2006.09.001 |s2cid=18202551 }}</ref> and self-promotion<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Levashina |first1=Julia |last2=Hartwell |first2=Christopher J. |last3=Morgeson |first3=Frederick P. |last4=Campion |first4=Michael A. |title=The Structured Employment Interview: Narrative and Quantitative Review of the Research Literature |journal=Personnel Psychology |date=March 2014 |volume=67 |issue=1 |pages=241–293 |doi=10.1111/peps.12052 }}</ref> as further explanations that can contribute to differences in pay despite uniform performance. Managers ability to identify the role of biases in perpetuating inequalities within the workplace can be instrumental in improving firm outcomes.

=== Game Theory ===
Game theory is the study where individuals study the different choices agents make with respect to their personal preferences, incentives and benefits.<ref>{{cite book |doi=10.4324/9780203140000 |title=Plato and Education (RLE Edu K) |date=2012 |last1=Barrow |first1=Robin |isbn=978-1-136-49475-8 }}{{page needed|date=April 2024}}</ref>

==== Assumptions of game theory ====

# Common Knowledge: Common knowledge is the assumption that information presented in the game is known by all players taking part in the game.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Brandenburger |first=Adam |date=23 April 2021 |title=The Role of Common Knowledge Assumptions in Game Theory |url=https://cet.econ.northwestern.edu/dekel/pdf/role-of-common-knowledge-assumptions-in-game-theory.pdf }}</ref>
# Rationality: The rational assumption is one where players are able to accurately weigh the cost and benefits of all the information presented subsequently making their decisions based on their assessment.<ref name="Ross 2021">{{Citation |last=Ross |first=Don |title=Game Theory |date=2021 |url=https://plato.stanford.edu/archives/fall2021/entries/game-theory/ |encyclopedia=The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy |editor-last=Zalta |editor-first=Edward N. |access-date=2023-04-23 |edition=Fall 2021 |publisher=Metaphysics Research Lab, Stanford University}}</ref> Economic rationality in game theory is the assumption that the player ranks their assessments based on what would benefit them the most. Subsequently the player will choose the outcome as well as paths that best benefit themselves.<ref name="Ross 2021"/>

===== Strategies in Game Theory =====

# Best response: The best response is the strategy (or strategies) that yields the most favourable outcome for players, with the assumption that the players have been given other strategies.<ref>{{cite book |last1=Fudenberg |first1=Drew |last2=Tirole |first2=Jean |title=Game Theory |date=1991 |publisher=MIT Press |isbn=978-0-262-06141-4 }}{{page needed|date=April 2024}}</ref>
# Nash Equilibrium: The nash equilibrium is the strategy where players have no incentive to deviate from their choices or behaviours.<ref>{{cite book |doi=10.1016/B0-08-043076-7/02230-0 |chapter=Game Theory: Noncooperative Games |title=International Encyclopedia of the Social & Behavioral Sciences |date=2001 |last1=Van Damme |first1=E. |pages=5873–5880 |isbn=978-0-08-043076-8 |chapter-url=https://research.tilburguniversity.edu/en/publications/ff518f2b-501f-4d99-817b-c2ba146d3d41 }}</ref>
# Dominant Strategy: The strategy where no matter what the other player plays the dominant strategy will always be superior producing and dictating the outcome.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Dominant Strategy |url=https://corporatefinanceinstitute.com/resources/capital-markets/dominant-strategy/ |access-date=2023-04-23 |website=Corporate Finance Institute |language=en-US}}</ref> '''Strictly dominated strategy''' is a strategy where the other strategy chosen always results in a lousier outcome.<ref name="builtin.com">{{Cite web |title=Dominated Strategy in Game Theory Explained {{!}} Built In |url=https://builtin.com/data-science/dominated-strategy-in-game-theory |access-date=2023-04-23 |website=builtin.com |language=en}}</ref> '''Weakly dominated strategy''' is a strategy where it produces the lousier or equivalent outcome when compared to the outcome of the alternate strategy.<ref name="builtin.com"/>

===== Examples of games in Game Theory =====

# Prisoners dilemma: The prisoner dilemma game provides economists with insights on disputes between individuals and group incentives that result in the outcomes being less than optimal.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Kingston |first=Michael |date=4 Nov 2022 |title=Dominated Strategy in Game Theory Explained |url=https://builtin.com/data-science/dominated-strategy-in-game-theory |access-date=23 Apr 2023}}</ref>
# Simultaneous games: The simultaneous game is one where players make decisions at the same time.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Sun |first1=Chia-Hung |title=Simultaneous and sequential choice in a symmetric two-player game with canyon-shaped payoffs |journal=The Japanese Economic Review |date=April 2020 |volume=71 |issue=2 |pages=191–219 |doi=10.1007/s42973-019-00011-0 |s2cid=220505322 }}</ref>

== Demand Analysis and Forecasting ==

[[Demand forecasting]] assists management in predicting future sales and revenue projection, which inform operations and marketing decisions as well future financial planning.<ref name=":0">{{cite book |last1=McGuigan |first1=James R. |last2=Moyer |first2=R. Charles |last3=Harris |first3=Frederick H. deB |title=Managerial Economics: Applications, Strategies and Tactics |date=2016 |publisher=Cengage Learning |isbn=978-1-305-50638-1 }}{{page needed|date=April 2024}}</ref> The process of demand forecasting often uses business analytics, particularly [[predictive analytics]], with respect to historical data and other analytical information, to make an accurate estimation. For example, using an estimate of a firm's capital expenditure and cash flow, managers can create forecasts that assist in financial planning and improve the financial health of the firm.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Demand Forecasting: An Industry Guide |url=https://www.demandcaster.com/demand-forecasting-an-industry-guide/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200927230025/https://www.demandcaster.com/demand-forecasting-an-industry-guide/ |archive-date=2020-09-27 |access-date=2022-05-03 |language=en-US}}</ref>

Effective demand management considers factors which are both within and beyond the firm's control, such as disposable income, competition, price, advertising and customer service.<ref name=":0" />

[[Consumer choice]] is highly influential on demand analysis, as each consumer aims to maximise their satisfaction with a combination of goods and services, subject to their personal budget constraint.<ref name=":0" /> Costs of production

[[Production (economics)|Production]] costs directly affect a firm's profitability. In order to maximise profits, firms identify the cost minimising output level for a firm where marginal cost equals marginal revenue. The most common types of costs that are factored into this decision include:<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.investopedia.com/terms/p/production-cost.asp |access-date=2022-05-03 |title=Production costs |language=en-US}}</ref>
*[[Fixed costs]]
*[[Variable costs]]
*[[Marginal cost]]
*[[Average total cost]] 
*[[Sunk costs]]
The impact of short-run and long run costs are important in determining production in a certain firm
. It is assumed some costs are fixed in the short-run and are thus considered "fixed costs". Thus production costs are determined by variable costs. However, in the long run, all costs are variable, which allows more flexibility in changing inputs to determine the optimal level of inputs for a profit maximising output.<ref name=":7" />

== Profitability Management ==

Profitability management is understanding what makes a firm profitable, and what can be done to improve its profitability.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www2.deloitte.com/ch/en/pages/financial-services/solutions/cost-and-profitability-management.html |access-date=2022-05-02| website=Deloitte |language=en-US|title=Cost and Profitability management &#124; Deloitte Switzerland &#124; Financial Services &#124; Banking &#124; Services }}</ref> It integrates finance and sales, and aims to optimize sales revenue and marginal cost of the firm. Profit management is technology enabled, as firms must be quick to respond to rapid changing market and to know the true economic cost of its products and services. Management needs to drive cooperation between different functions of the firm such as sales, marketing, and finance, to ensure the teams recognize the importance of coordinated effort. Proper planning and profitability management is key to good business management.<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.ventanaresearch.com/focus/officeoffinance/profitabilitymanagement |title=profitability management |access-date=2022-05-03 |language=en-US |archive-date=2022-05-22 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220522223155/https://www.ventanaresearch.com/focus/officeoffinance/profitabilitymanagement |url-status=dead }}</ref>

== Capital Management ==

Capital management is the planning, monitoring, and controlling of the assets and liabilities of a firm, particularly, in an effort to maintain cash flow to meet the firm's short-term and long-term financial obligations. Proper capital management is important to the financial health of a firm, with efficient resource allocation through capital management, firms can improve its cash flow and profitability. Capital management involves tracking various ratios within the firm, most important ones include:<ref>{{Cite web |access-date=2022-05-03 |title=Working capital management |url=https://www.investopedia.com/terms/w/workingcapitalmanagement.asp |language=en-US |website=Investopedia}}</ref>

*[[Capital ratio]]
*[[Inventory turnover ratio]]
*[[Collection ratio]]

Rate of return and cost of capital (i.e. interest rate) are important factors of capital management.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |date=2015-05-08 |title=Scope of Managerial Economics |url=https://www.economicsdiscussion.net/business-economics/scope-of-managerial-economics/7122 |access-date=2022-03-22 |website=Economics Discussion |language=en-US}}</ref>

==Implications of macroeconomics and microeconomics==

When making decisions, managerial economics is used to analyze the micro and macroeconomic environments relating to an organization. [[Microeconomics]] considers the actions of individual firms surrounding [[utility]] maximization, whilst [[Macroeconomics]] considers the actions and behaviour of the economy as a whole.<ref name="MSQ">{{cite web |title=Managerial Economics and Micro Economics |url=https://www.managementstudyguide.com/managerial-micro-economics.htm |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=MSG}}</ref> As such, both area of economics have influence in the development of managerial economic frameworks.

=== Macroeconomics ===
With regard to [[macroeconomic]] trends, the forecasting and analysis of areas such as output, unemployment, inflation and societal issues are essential in managerial economics.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Hall |first1=Mary |title=Explaining the World Through Macroeconomic Analysis |url=https://www.investopedia.com/insights/macroeconomic-analysis/ |website=Investopedia |access-date=22 April 2021}}</ref> This is because these areas in the macroeconomy have the ability to provide an overview of global market conditions, which can be imperative for managers to understand.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Johnston |first1=Kevin |title=The Effects of Macro and Microeconomics in Decision Making |url=https://smallbusiness.chron.com/effects-macro-microeconomics-decision-making-35035.html |website=Chron |access-date=22 April 2021}}</ref> 
An example of managerial economics using macroeconomic principles is a manager choosing to hire new staff rather than training old ones in a time when the rate of unemployment is high, as the possible talent pool would be very large.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Bloom |first1=Nicholas |last2=Van Reenen |date=2010 |title=Why Do Management Practices Differ across Firms and Countries? |url=https://learn-ap-southeast-2-prod-fleet01-xythos.content.blackboardcdn.com/5fd17f67f4120/5687725?X-Blackboard-Expiration=1651503600000&X-Blackboard-Signature=p%2BBF4FsSrMc%2FBtBRQ5Hl8HV7ZUjrWL7fkw%2FHucKUnzA%3D&X-Blackboard-Client-Id=149017&response-cache-control=private%2C%20max-age%3D21600&response-content-disposition=inline%3B%20filename%2A%3DUTF-8%27%27jep.24.1.203.pdf&response-content-type=application%2Fpdf&X-Amz-Security-Token=IQoJb3JpZ2luX2VjEHkaDmFwLXNvdXRoZWFzdC0yIkYwRAIgFXyQBkol7mgbwfsVfZ3eX7gKKqqRxTK%2Bikjk%2Bl2EoIkCIBOkfTgmpEfUzfJgo9i%2BhaRrdFBxjv0XzN0ExE7nTHwcKuEECEIQABoMNTU2OTAzODYxMzYxIgwV2%2FPN4bNND4wphA4qvgSLhfGtONuz0XsOC%2BwLnNTkZ8LBc3CZDM69G%2BB0qeFNV74ObzJYskGxQ%2Fds%2FUVv7C3S4cJcfhIi8fbYooK31J%2FE8Gadp1%2F4UpnAw15L4A33P43R9UIOA1gjJEsZ%2BqpmkFNxhswn4is4oMYLuiQma4ONigvDoEDx020LwkQkSLbI%2BMcj1YehQcGxDjmZrtuseXrDrWZbL1qcAvwvr2TGGbbVm%2Fa3sCeJRWPaIm5kpIIwNugiB8A7jZQNaExVYb8mE0xKJWdx5JnROJ%2Fd9bwJbJ69qOrXNUg47lYK1FzU8YykpJi7jRlyDt3c0ycHTX9Lk67vdVf1c77NaVsGGj8NcnZV3xBi6MdWXcFK3%2B3Jbvj8rddRarhkLXZkAFEiLvybpb25oXEVVtoHyWhip6Ur7bOiR9PoXhiWFVEND9puLILNbl1rBseheNR1h8BAcXPPgHaMDbOfhnpZWJgi4RhxpWZkdFHDClrZ%2FFKI8n%2F5mYdZO33%2F1F0ZCpyBhMbFWOM6TsjtapYozQLwmLn1xYbWeC2eeWtzmGdSK14kYhuiFjyqIqJzgrfjgmoPod78ljtYT0npOOabN4ubjHu4haj8b1QUg4%2F5ftNgYEDa51mo9U0ki%2BheVENwSjyHnbCUWa2KZjZMn4%2FXof0CIk2COrsgOpOaTg4YsHdz3bXbE8vUfL7ZcGeinTGhi1n4Y4Zig02X%2Fr9JdChLgnYtC7mAsW6yqo%2Bf0uA%2Fjvh7YvzuEA9dR89uDhBkZ%2FmpRZo5bLUSgIuUMOrDvpMGOqoB1qte91zVYHv%2FLUrj84paKtkJloPfMTNv%2FUMdcNEloZkKNk6fSjVW9uTsBM0GlnYOdfDocIqbSiAFNwMIVLuhbd8m5yW0Ow1PRQjdKMHqF0zypj2maML2EfgVayCEdQntmaqkdqo6KScat63ymt4cxloa0BeeSxsllKERvrYktJdIKxaJgmsxSNPUXhaZwwsdqpdV7IIuUFKG3JMxmzFAXvAN71%2FCGZ7o78A%3D&X-Amz-Algorithm=AWS4-HMAC-SHA256&X-Amz-Date=20220502T090000Z&X-Amz-SignedHeaders=host&X-Amz-Expires=21600&X-Amz-Credential=ASIAYDKQORRYRVD64UUN%2F20220502%2Fap-southeast-2%2Fs3%2Faws4_request&X-Amz-Signature=941f7fd7c2f13b36b9cd5bc422e238779d98a4719a5078e2acae091dabb6e4d1  |journal=Journal of Economic Perspectives |volume=24 |pages=203–224|doi=10.1257/jep.24.1.203 |doi-access=free }}</ref> The political structure of a country (whether authoritarian or democratic), political stability and attitudes towards the private sector can also affect the growth and development of organizations.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Bondarenko |first1=Peter |title=Macroeconomics |url=https://www.britannica.com/topic/macroeconomics |website=Britannica |access-date=22 April 2021}}</ref> This can be seen through the influence different government policies can have on management quality.<ref name=":4" /> In particular, policies around product market competition has been seen to significantly impact collective management practices in countries by either reducing or supporting poorly managed firms.<ref name=":4" /> A clear understanding of relevant markets and their different conditions is a vital task for a managerial economist, as even with market instability and fluctuations the goal is to always steer the company to profits.<ref name=":3" />

'''Managerial economics has components of microeconomics'''

Managers study and manage the internal environment of organisations and work for the profitability and long-term operation of the organisation. This aspect refers to the study of microeconomics. Managerial economics deals with the problems individual organisations face, such as the organisation's main objectives, the demand for its products, the organisation's price and output decisions, available substitutes and giveaways, the supply of inputs and raw materials, the target or potential consumers of its products, etc.<ref name="distanceeducationju.in"/>

=== [[Microeconomics]] ===

Microeconomics is closely related to Managerial economics through areas such as; consumer demand and supply, opportunity cost, revenue creation and cost minimization.<ref name="MSQ" /> Managerial economics inculcates the application of microeconomics application and makes use of economic theories and methods in analyzing a business and its management. Moreover, managerial economics combines economic tool and technique to solve the managerial problems.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Muh. Fahrurrozi |first1=ozi |last2=Purnama putra |first2=putra |last3=Tiris Sudrartono |first3=Sudrartono |last4=Eka Hendrayani |first4=Hendrayani |last5=Lisawanto |first5=Lisawanto |title=Managerial Economics in Managerial Decision Making |journal=Turkish Online Journal of Qualitative Inquiry |date=5 June 2021 |volume=12 |url=https://eprints.hamzanwadi.ac.id/4737/ }}</ref>

Microeconomics also gives indication on the most effective allocation of resources the business has available.<ref>{{cite web |title=Microeconomics |url=https://corporatefinanceinstitute.com/resources/knowledge/economics/microeconomics/ |website=Corporate Finance Institute |access-date=22 April 2021}}</ref>
These microeconomic theories and considerations are used via managerial economics to make decisions regarding the business. By understanding the principles of microeconomics, managers can be well informed to make accurate decisions regarding the firm.<ref name="MSQ" />

An example of managerial economics using microeconomic principles is the decision of a manager to increase the price of the goods being sold. A manager should evaluate the price elasticity of the product to equate the respective demand of the product after the price change.<ref name="MSQ" />

==Managerial economics in practice==

From a management perspective, managerial economics techniques are useful in many areas regarding business decision-making, most commonly including:

* [[Risk analysis]] – various models are used to quantify [[risk]] and asymmetric [[Information economics|information]] and to employ them in [[decision theory|decision rules]] to manage risk.<ref>{{cite web |first1=Michael R. |last1=Greenberg |first2=Louis |last2=Anthony |title=INTRODUCTION TO SPECIAL ISSUE: ECONOMICS OF RISK ANALYSIS |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/page/journal/15396924/homepage/virtual_issue__economics_of_risk_analysis.htm }}</ref>
* [[Production (economics)|Production]] analysis – microeconomic techniques are used to analyze<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Kurz |first1=Heinz D. |last2=Salvadori |first2=Neri |title=Production Theory: an Introduction |journal=The Indian Economic Journal |date=June 2000 |volume=47 |issue=4 |pages=15–29 |doi=10.1177/0019466220000402 }}</ref> [[production theory basics|production efficiency]], optimum factor allocation, [[cost]]s, [[economies of scale]] and to estimate the firm's cost function.
* Pricing analysis – microeconomic techniques are used to analyze various [[pricing|pricing decisions]] including [[transfer pricing]], [[joint product pricing]], [[price discrimination]], price elasticity estimations, and choosing the optimum pricing method.<ref>{{cite web |title=Pricing Analytics The three-minute guide |url=https://www2.deloitte.com/content/dam/Deloitte/global/Documents/Deloitte-Analytics/dttl-analytics-us-da-pricinganalytics3minguide.pdf |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160418070258/https://www.deloitte.com/content/dam/Deloitte/global/Documents/Deloitte-Analytics/dttl-analytics-us-da-pricinganalytics3minguide.pdf |archive-date=2016-04-18 |access-date=22 April 2021 |website=Deloitte}}</ref>
* [[Capital budgeting]] – investment theory is used to examine a firm's [[Corporate finance#The investment decision|capital purchasing decisions]].<ref>{{cite web |last1=Kenton |first1=Will |title=Capital Budgeting |url=https://www.investopedia.com/terms/c/capitalbudgeting.asp |website=Investopedia |access-date=22 April 2021}}</ref>
